revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]Created page with '{{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member o...'
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets.
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
== References == *信楽香仁『天狗の山 くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27 鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科 日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系 京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典 京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館
[[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 8th century]] [[Category:772 establishments]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Kurama-dera
鞍馬寺 | img = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | img_size = | img_capt = The main building | venerated = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | denomination = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | founded = 772? | closed = | founder = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | teacher = | director = | abbot = | priest = | rinpoche = | reverend = | address = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | country = [[Japan]] }}{{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]]. Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]]. ==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area. The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship. ==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord. == References == *信楽香仁『天狗の山　くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27　鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科　日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系　京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典　京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館 [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 8th century]] [[Category:772 establishments]] [[ko:구라마 사]] [[zh:鞍馬寺]] [[ja:鞍馬寺]]

However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord. ==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it, but does not appear in most English language guidebooks. == References == *信楽香仁『天狗の山　くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27　鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科　日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系　京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典　京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館 {{Commonscat|Kurama-dera}} [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 8th century]] [[Category:772 establishments]] [[ko:구라마 사]] [[zh:鞍馬寺]] [[ja:鞍馬寺]]

Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition ==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940. == References == *信楽香仁『天狗の山　くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27　鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科　日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系　京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典　京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館 ==External links== * {{ja icon}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] (Kurama-dera itself has no homepage) {{Commonscat|Kurama-dera}} {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}} [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in the 8th century]] [[Category:770 establishments]]

It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]]. Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]]. ==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area. The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship. ==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]]. The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord. ==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it, but does not appear in most English language guidebooks. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].

Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.

It does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>

{{short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main building | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it{{elucidate|date=December 2016}}, but does not appear in most English language guidebooks. It does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.
== References == {{Reflist}} *信楽香仁『天狗の山 くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27 鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科 日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系 京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典 京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館
==External links== {{Commons category|Kurama-dera}} * {{ja icon}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] * {{ja icon}} [http://www.kuramadera.or.jp/ Kurama-dera homepage] {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}}

Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.
== References == {{Reflist}} *信楽香仁『天狗の山 くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27 鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科 日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系 京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典 京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館
==External links== {{Commons category|Kurama-dera}} * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.kuramadera.or.jp/ Kurama-dera homepage]

The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>

{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.
== References == {{Reflist}} *信楽香仁『天狗の山 くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27 鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科 日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系 京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典 京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館
==External links== {{Commons category|Kurama-dera}} * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.kuramadera.or.jp/ Kurama-dera homepage]

Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>

{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.
== References == {{Reflist}} *信楽香仁『天狗の山 くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27 鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科 日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系 京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典 京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館
==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category|Kurama-dera}} * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.kuramadera.or.jp/ Kurama-dera homepage]

It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category|Kurama-dera}} * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.kuramadera.or.jp/ Kurama-dera homepage]

{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category|Kurama-dera}} * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.kuramadera.or.jp/ Kurama-dera homepage]

{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>

{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>

It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.

However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.
== References == {{Reflist}} *信楽香仁『天狗の山 くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27 鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科 日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系 京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典 京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館
==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Kurama-dera]][[Category:kk]][[Category|Kurama-dera}} * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.kuramadera.or.jp/ Kurama-dera homepage]
