{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[:en:Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.
== References == {{Reflist}} *信楽香仁『天狗の山 くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27 鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科 日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系 京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典 京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館
==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Kurama-dera}} * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.kuramadera.or.jp/ Kurama-dera homepage]

{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] which houses some [[National Treasures of Japan]]. It was a member of the [[Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[:en:Jianzhen|Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[:en:Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[:en:Kurama-dera Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.
== References == {{Reflist}} *信楽香仁『天狗の山 くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27 鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科 日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系 京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典 京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館
==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Kurama-dera}} * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.kuramadera.or.jp/ Kurama-dera homepage]
{{Authority control}} {{Coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title}}

}}

It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.

==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] which houses some [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]]. It was a member of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[:en:Jianzhen|Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[:en:Mount Kurama|Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[:en:Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[:en:Kurama-dera Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[:en:Venus|Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

{{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Kurama Kōkyō|qid=Q11663912}}|prefecture={{ill|Sakyō-ku|qid=Q1321832}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.11806111|N|135.77074167|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Fujiwara no Isendo|qid=Q11623016}}|part_of={{ill|New Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qid=Q11504057}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q608668]] Wikidata name: Kurama-dera [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] which houses some [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]]. It was a member of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[:en:Jianzhen|Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[:en:Mount Kurama|Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[:en:Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[:en:Kurama-dera Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[:en:Venus|Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

It was a member of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[:en:Jianzhen|Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[:en:Mount Kurama|Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[:en:Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[:en:Kurama-dera Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[:en:Venus|Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.
== References == {{Reflist}} *信楽香仁『天狗の山 くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27 鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科 日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系 京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典 京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館
==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Kurama-dera}} * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.kuramadera.or.jp/ Kurama-dera homepage]

The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كوراما ديرا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kurama-dera]][[de:Kurama-dera]][[en:Kurama-dera]][[fa:کوراما-درا]][[fr:Kurama-dera]][[it:Kurama-dera]][[ja:鞍馬寺]][[ko:구라마데라]][[my:ခုရမကျောင်းတော်]][[zh:鞍馬寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Kurama Kōkyō|WD=Q11663912}}|prefecture={{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.11806111|N|135.77074167|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Fujiwara no Isendo|WD=Q11623016}}|part_of={{ill|New Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q11504057}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q608668]] Wikidata name: Kurama-dera [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] which houses some [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]]. It was a member of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[:en:Jianzhen|Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[:en:Mount Kurama|Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[:en:Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[:en:Kurama-dera Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[:en:Venus|Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Kurama-dera}} * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.kuramadera.or.jp/ Kurama-dera homepage]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كوراما ديرا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kurama-dera]][[de:Kurama-dera]][[en:Kurama-dera]][[fa:کوراما-درا]][[fr:Kurama-dera]][[it:Kurama-dera]][[ja:鞍馬寺]][[ko:구라마데라]][[my:ခုရမကျောင်းတော်]][[zh:鞍馬寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Kurama Kōkyō|WD=Q11663912}}|prefecture={{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.11806111|N|135.77074167|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Fujiwara no Isendo|WD=Q11623016}}|part_of={{ill|New Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q11504057}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q608668]] Wikidata name: Kurama-dera [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Vaiśravaṇa]], [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] which houses some [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]]. It was a member of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[:en:Jianzhen|Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[:en:Mount Kurama|Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[:en:Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[:en:Kurama-dera Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[:en:Venus|Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.
== References == {{Reflist}} *信楽香仁『天狗の山 くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27 鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科 日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系 京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典 京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館
==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Kurama-dera}} * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.kuramadera.or.jp/ Kurama-dera homepage]

Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[:en:Mount Kurama|Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[:en:Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[:en:Kurama-dera Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]].

Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[:en:Venus|Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.

Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] which houses some [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]]. It was a member of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[:en:Jianzhen|Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[:en:Mount Kurama|Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[:en:Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[:en:Kurama-dera Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[:en:Venus|Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>

}} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] which houses some [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]]. It was a member of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and subordinate to [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]] from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of [[:en:Jianzhen|Jianzhen]].
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of [[:en:Mount Kurama|Mount Kurama]], it is accessible by its own [[:en:Aerial lift|cable car]] line, the [[:en:Kurama-dera Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]].
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]] and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]], protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from [[:en:Venus|Venus]] 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''[[:en:Xian (Taoism)|xian]]''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

ديرا]][[sn:commons:Category:Kurama-dera]][[de:Kurama-dera]][[en:Kurama-dera]][[fa:کوراما-درا]][[fr:Kurama-dera]][[it:Kurama-dera]][[ja:鞍馬寺]][[ko:구라마데라]][[my:ခုရမကျောင်းတော်]][[zh:鞍馬寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Kurama Kōkyō|WD=Q11663912}}|prefecture={{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.11806111|N|135.77074167|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Fujiwara no Isendo|WD=Q11623016}}|part_of={{ill|New Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q11504057}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q608668]] Wikidata name: Kurama-dera [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Vaiśravaṇa}}, [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} which houses some {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)}}. It was a member of the {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} sect and subordinate to {{ill|Shōren-in|en|Shōren-in|WD=Q377386|lt=Shōren-in}} from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of {{ill|Jianzhen|en|Jianzhen|WD=Q714061|lt=Jianzhen}}.
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of {{ill|Mount Kurama|en|Mount Kurama|WD=Q410132|lt=Mount Kurama}}, it is accessible by its own {{ill|Aerial lift|en|Aerial lift|WD=Q13409250|lt=cable car}} line, the {{ill|Kurama-dera Cable|en|Kurama-dera Cable|WD=Q6445551|lt=Kurama-dera Cable}}.
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu}} and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}}, protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from {{ill|Venus|en|Venus|WD=Q313|lt=Venus}} 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''{{ill|Xian (Taoism)|en|Xian (Taoism)|WD=Q1207451|lt=xian}}''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu}} and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}}, protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from {{ill|Venus|en|Venus|WD=Q313|lt=Venus}} 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''{{ill|Xian (Taoism)|en|Xian (Taoism)|WD=Q1207451|lt=xian}}''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

}} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} which houses some {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)}}. It was a member of the {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} sect and subordinate to {{ill|Shōren-in|en|Shōren-in|WD=Q377386|lt=Shōren-in}} from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of {{ill|Mount Kurama|en|Mount Kurama|WD=Q410132|lt=Mount Kurama}}, it is accessible by its own {{ill|Aerial lift|en|Aerial lift|WD=Q13409250|lt=cable car}} line, the {{ill|Kurama-dera Cable|en|Kurama-dera Cable|WD=Q6445551|lt=Kurama-dera Cable}}.

}} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} which houses some {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)}}. It was a member of the {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} sect and subordinate to {{ill|Shōren-in|en|Shōren-in|WD=Q377386|lt=Shōren-in}} from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of {{ill|Jianzhen|en|Jianzhen|WD=Q714061|lt=Jianzhen}}.
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of {{ill|Mount Kurama|en|Mount Kurama|WD=Q410132|lt=Mount Kurama}}, it is accessible by its own {{ill|Aerial lift|en|Aerial lift|WD=Q13409250|lt=cable car}} line, the {{ill|Kurama-dera Cable|en|Kurama-dera Cable|WD=Q6445551|lt=Kurama-dera Cable}}.
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu}} and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}}, protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from {{ill|Venus|en|Venus|WD=Q313|lt=Venus}} 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''{{ill|Xian (Taoism)|en|Xian (Taoism)|WD=Q1207451|lt=xian}}''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.
== References == {{Reflist}} *信楽香仁『天狗の山 くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27 鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科 日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系 京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典 京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館
==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Kurama-dera}} * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.kuramadera.or.jp/ Kurama-dera homepage]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كوراما ديرا]][[co:commons:Category:Kurama-dera]][[de:Kurama-dera]][[en:Kurama-dera]][[fa:کوراما-درا]][[fr:Kurama-dera]][[it:Kurama-dera]][[ja:鞍馬寺]][[ko:구라마데라]][[my:ခုရမကျောင်းတော်]][[zh:鞍馬寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كوراما ديرا]][[co:commons:Category:Kurama-dera]][[de:Kurama-dera]][[en:Kurama-dera]][[fa:کوراما-درا]][[fr:Kurama-dera]][[it:Kurama-dera]][[ja:鞍馬寺]][[ko:구라마데라]][[my:ခုရမကျောင်းတော်]][[zh:鞍馬寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كوراما ديرا]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kurama-dera]][[de:Kurama-dera]][[en:Kurama-dera]][[fa:کوراما-درا]][[fr:Kurama-dera]][[it:Kurama-dera]][[ja:鞍馬寺]][[ko:구라마데라]][[my:ခုရမကျောင်းတော်]][[zh:鞍馬寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Kurama Kōkyō|WD=Q11663912|1=Kurama Kōkyō}}|prefecture={{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.11806111|N|135.77074167|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Fujiwara no Isendo|WD=Q11623016|1=Fujiwara no Isendo}}|part_of={{ill|New Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q11504057|1=New Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}}} {{Short description|Temple in Kyoto prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kurama-dera | native_name = 鞍馬寺 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Kurama-dera sanmon.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = The main entrance | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Sakyō-ku Kurama Honmachi 1074, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|7|5.02|N|135|46|14.67|E|scale:20000}} | religious_affiliation = Independent (Kuramakōkyō) | deity = Sonten (union of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Vaiśravaṇa|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}, [[Kannon]], Defender Lord) | country = {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = Jiànzhēn (鑑禎) | year_completed = 770? }} {{nihongo|'''Kurama-dera'''|鞍馬寺}} is a temple in the far north of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} which houses some {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}. It was a member of the {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} sect and subordinate to {{ill|Shōren-in|en|Shōren-in|WD=Q377386|lt=Shōren-in|1=Shōren-in}} from the 12th century until 1949 when it founded its own religious body. The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of {{ill|Jianzhen|en|Jianzhen|WD=Q714061|lt=Jianzhen|1=Jianzhen}}.
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of {{ill|Mount Kurama|en|Mount Kurama|WD=Q410132|lt=Mount Kurama|1=Mount Kurama}}, it is accessible by its own {{ill|Aerial lift|en|Aerial lift|WD=Q13409250|lt=cable car|1=Aerial lift}} line, the {{ill|Kurama-dera Cable|en|Kurama-dera Cable|WD=Q6445551|lt=Kurama-dera Cable|1=Kurama-dera Cable}}.
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu|1=Tengu}} and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}, protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from {{ill|Venus|en|Venus|WD=Q313|lt=Venus|1=Venus}} 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''{{ill|Xian (Taoism)|en|Xian (Taoism)|WD=Q1207451|lt=xian|1=Xian (Taoism)}}''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.

The object of worship is esoteric and unique to the temple. It is said to have been founded by a disciple of {{ill|Jianzhen|en|Jianzhen|WD=Q714061|lt=Jianzhen|1=Jianzhen}}.
Situated in secluded wilderness at the base of {{ill|Mount Kurama|en|Mount Kurama|WD=Q410132|lt=Mount Kurama|1=Mount Kurama}}, it is accessible by its own {{ill|Aerial lift|en|Aerial lift|WD=Q13409250|lt=cable car|1=Aerial lift}} line, the {{ill|Kurama-dera Cable|en|Kurama-dera Cable|WD=Q6445551|lt=Kurama-dera Cable|1=Kurama-dera Cable}}.
==History== The temple was founded in the 8th century AD. Its origins are historically unclear, but it is said that the Chinese monk Jianzhen initiated a disciple into the Buddhist path, who saw in a dream in 772 that Mount Kurama had a spiritual power and built an esoteric temple to concentrate and control this power. It was burned down many times throughout the medieval era but the Buddhist statues and treasures inside it were always rescued and are today National Treasures. It is still believed today that {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu|1=Tengu}} and other mountain spirits live in this area.
The temple switched between three different Buddhist sects over the centuries. Finally, in the postwar era, abbot Kouun Shigaraki founded his own religion and split the temple away from Buddhism. Thus the temple was finally able to reconcile with its ''[[yamabushi]]'' patrons and other unaffiliated, esoteric adherents of mountain worship.
==Object of worship== The original objects of worship under the Tendai order were {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}, protector of the north, and the Thousand-Armed [[Kannon]]. However, in the worship hall another statue has been added to these two, which the current leadership says creates a trinity called the "Sonten". Bishamonten represents the Sun, Kannon represents Love, and the third statue, the Defender Lord, represents Power, together being the "Spiritual Kings of the World". The Defender Lord came from {{ill|Venus|en|Venus|WD=Q313|lt=Venus|1=Venus}} 6.5 million years ago and is eternally 16 years old due to chemical differences in the atmospheres of the two planets. Some New Age writers identify the Defender Lord with [[Sanat Kumara]].
The actual objects of worship are ordinarily concealed from view, but visitors can see replicas of all three images in the main building. The replica of the Defender Lord depicts a ''{{ill|Xian (Taoism)|en|Xian (Taoism)|WD=Q1207451|lt=xian|1=Xian (Taoism)}}''-like figure with a long beard, a big nose, wings, and a halo made of leaves. It is thought that the tengu in the area proceed from this Defender Lord.
==Tourism== It is an extremely popular temple for Japanese people to visit, owing to the many mysteries and occult events surrounding it. Mt. Kurama is considered to be a mystical place because of the appearance of what are called Tengu, long-nosed legendary creatures found in Japanese folk stories that represented to ancient people the mysterious power of mountains. Mt. Kurama is believed to be the birthplace of reiki, a type of “energy therapy”.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/places-go/kurama-dera/ | title=Kurama-dera }}</ref> The temple, now strongly affiliated with the mountain-ascetics, does appear in the 14th edition of Lonely Planet Japan as a Kyoto Highlight.<ref>Lonely Planet Japan 14th edition</ref>
==Yuki-jinja== [[File:Kuramadera1.jpg|thumb|right|Yuki-jinja]] There is a famous {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} on the grounds of the temple called Yuki-jinja, which was founded in the year 940.
== References == {{Reflist}} *信楽香仁『天狗の山 くらまだより』、大東出版社、1990 *井上靖、塚本善隆監修、遠藤周作、信楽香仁著『古寺巡礼京都27 鞍馬寺』、淡交社、1978 *竹村俊則『昭和京都名所図会 洛北』駸々堂、1982 *『週刊朝日百科 日本の国宝』13号（鞍馬寺ほか）、朝日新聞社、1997 *『日本歴史地名大系 京都市の地名』、平凡社 *『角川日本地名大辞典 京都府』、角川書店 *『国史大辞典』、吉川弘文館
==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] [[Category|Kurama-dera}} * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.yukijinjya.jp/ Yuki-jinja homepage] * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.kuramadera.or.jp/ Kurama-dera homepage]
